expanded_description: A comprehensive guide to writing chemical formulas from systematic names, covering the reverse nomenclature process for all compound types including ionic compounds (Type I and Type II), polyatomic ions, covalent compounds, acids, bases, and hydrates.

core_concept.principle: Writing formulas from names is the reverse of naming: identify compound type from name patterns, determine ion charges or atom counts, and combine components with subscripts that balance charges or match prefixes.
core_concept.explanation: Formula writing requires recognizing naming patterns that reveal compound type: names with Roman numerals indicate Type II ionic compounds; names with Greek prefixes indicate covalent compounds; names ending in '-ic acid' or '-ous acid' indicate oxyacids; names with 'hydro-' prefix indicate binary acids; names ending in 'hydrate' indicate hydrates. Once compound type is identified, apply the appropriate rules: for ionic compounds, balance cation and anion charges to zero; for covalent compounds, use prefixes as subscripts; for acids, combine H⁺ with the appropriate anion; for hydrates, add dot notation with water count. This systematic approach enables accurate formula writing for any systematically named compound.

image.alt: Flowchart showing decision tree for writing formulas from names based on name patterns
image.caption: Formula writing decision tree: identify compound type from name patterns, then apply appropriate rules.

sections.section_title: The Reverse Process: From Name to Formula
sections.introduction: While naming compounds converts formulas to words, writing formulas converts systematic names back to chemical symbols and subscripts. This reverse process is equally important for laboratory work, chemical calculations, and understanding chemical communication.

key_concept.definition: Writing formulas from names requires: (1) Identifying compound type from name patterns, (2) Determining ion charges or atom counts, (3) Combining components with correct subscripts to balance charges or match prefixes.
key_concept.context: Name patterns reveal compound type: Roman numerals = Type II ionic; Greek prefixes = covalent; 'hydro-' = binary acid; '-ic/-ous acid' = oxyacid; 'hydrate' = hydrate. Correct identification is the essential first step.

principles.statement: Specific name patterns indicate compound type: Roman numerals in parentheses indicate Type II ionic; Greek prefixes indicate covalent; 'hydro-' prefix indicates binary acid; '-ic/-ous acid' suffix indicates oxyacid; 'hydrate' suffix indicates hydrate.
principles.application: iron(III) chloride → Roman numeral → Type II ionic → Fe³⁺ + Cl⁻ → FeCl₃. carbon dioxide → Greek prefix → covalent → C + 2O → CO₂. hydrochloric acid → 'hydro-' → binary acid → H⁺ + Cl⁻ → HCl.
principles.example: Name analysis: 'copper(II) sulfate pentahydrate' → Roman numeral (Type II ionic) + polyatomic ion (sulfate) + hydrate prefix → Cu²⁺ + SO₄²⁻ + 5H₂O → CuSO₄·5H₂O.
principles.analogy: Like decoding a message: name patterns are the 'code' that tells you which rules to apply for formula writing.

image.alt: Chart showing name patterns and corresponding compound types
image.caption: Name patterns: Roman numeral = Type II ionic, prefixes = covalent, hydro- = binary acid, -ic/-ous = oxyacid.

principles.statement: For ionic compounds, the total positive charge must equal the total negative charge; subscripts are determined by balancing charges to zero, not by prefixes.
principles.application: aluminum oxide: Al³⁺ + O²⁻ → need 2 Al³⁺ (+6) and 3 O²⁻ (-6) → Al₂O₃. magnesium nitrate: Mg²⁺ + NO₃⁻ → need 2 NO₃⁻ to balance Mg²⁺ → Mg(NO₃)₂.
principles.example: Name: iron(III) sulfate. Analysis: Fe³⁺ (from III) + SO₄²⁻ → need 2 Fe³⁺ (+6) and 3 SO₄²⁻ (-6) → Fe₂(SO₄)₃. Parentheses needed for polyatomic ion with subscript > 1.

image.alt: Diagram showing charge balance method for ionic formula writing
image.caption: Charge balance: total positive charge must equal total negative charge for neutral compound.

principles.statement: For covalent compounds, Greek prefixes directly convert to subscripts: mono- = 1 (omit subscript), di- = ₂, tri- = ₃, tetra- = ₄, etc.
principles.application: dinitrogen tetroxide: di- = 2 N, tetra- = 4 O → N₂O₄. phosphorus pentachloride: no prefix on P = 1 P, penta- = 5 Cl → PCl₅.
principles.example: Name: sulfur hexafluoride. Analysis: no prefix on S = 1 S, hexa- = 6 F → SF₆. No charge balancing needed—prefixes give exact atom counts.

image.alt: Table showing Greek prefix to subscript conversion
image.caption: Prefix conversion: di- = ₂, tri- = ₃, tetra- = ₄, penta- = ₅, hexa- = ₆.

components.definition: Formula writing algorithm based on compound type
components.equation: $\text{Formula} = \begin{cases} \text{Ionic: Balance charges to zero} \\ \text{Covalent: Prefixes → subscripts} \\ \text{Acid: H⁺ + anion} \\ \text{Hydrate: Ionic · nH₂O} \end{cases}$
components.interpretation: First identify compound type from name pattern, then apply appropriate rules: ionic = charge balance, covalent = prefix conversion, acid = H⁺ + anion, hydrate = ionic + dot + water.

worked_examples.title: Identifying Compound Type from Name
worked_examples.problem: For each name, identify the compound type and the key information needed to write the formula: (a) sodium chloride, (b) iron(III) oxide, (c) dinitrogen pentoxide, (d) hydrofluoric acid, (e) copper(II) sulfate pentahydrate.

step_by_step.calculation: No Roman numeral, no prefixes, no 'hydro-', no 'acid', no 'hydrate' → Type I ionic. Na⁺ (Group 1) + Cl⁻ (Group 17) → charges balance 1:1
Roman numeral (III) → Type II ionic. Fe³⁺ (from III) + O²⁻ → need 2 Fe³⁺ (+6) and 3 O²⁻ (-6) for balance
Greek prefixes (di-, penta-) → covalent. di- = 2 N, penta- = 5 O → N₂O₅ (prefixes directly give subscripts)
'hydro-' prefix + 'acid' suffix → binary acid. H⁺ + F⁻ (fluoride) → HF (charges balance 1:1)
Roman numeral + polyatomic name + 'hydrate' → Type II ionic hydrate. Cu²⁺ + SO₄²⁻ + 5H₂O → CuSO₄·5H₂O

worked_examples.final_answer: (a) sodium chloride: Type I ionic, Na⁺ + Cl⁻ → NaCl. (b) iron(III) oxide: Type II ionic, Fe³⁺ + O²⁻ → Fe₂O₃. (c) dinitrogen pentoxide: covalent, prefixes → N₂O₅. (d) hydrofluoric acid: binary acid, H⁺ + F⁻ → HF. (e) copper(II) sulfate pentahydrate: Type II ionic hydrate, Cu²⁺ + SO₄²⁻ + 5H₂O → CuSO₄·5H₂O. Correct compound type identification is essential for accurate formula writing.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Identifying compound type from name patterns as first step in formula writing

sections.introduction: Ionic compound formula writing requires balancing cation and anion charges to zero. The process differs slightly for Type I (fixed-charge) and Type II (variable-charge) metals, but both follow the fundamental principle of charge balance.

key_concept.definition: For ionic compounds: (1) Identify cation and anion from name, (2) Determine their charges (from group number, Roman numeral, or memorized polyatomic ions), (3) Use subscripts to balance total positive and negative charges to zero, (4) Use parentheses around polyatomic ions when subscript > 1.
key_concept.context: Type I metals (Groups 1, 2, Al, Zn, Ag, Cd) have fixed charges determined by group number. Type II metals (most transition metals) have charges specified by Roman numerals in the name. Polyatomic ion charges must be memorized.

classifications.value: Metal name + nonmetal-ide or polyatomic name → balance charges using group number
characteristics: Parentheses for polyatomic ions with subscript > 1
classifications.behavior: Group 1 = +1, Group 2 = +2, Al = +3, Zn = +2, Ag = +1. Balance with anion charge to determine subscripts.

examples.explanation: Na⁺ (Group 1) + Cl⁻ (Group 17) → charges balance 1:1 → NaCl. No subscripts needed (subscript 1 omitted).

image.caption: NaCl: Na⁺ + Cl⁻ = 0, 1:1 ratio.

examples.explanation: Mg²⁺ (Group 2) + NO₃⁻ (memorized) → need 2 NO₃⁻ to balance Mg²⁺ → Mg(NO₃)₂. Parentheses needed for polyatomic ion with subscript > 1.

image.caption: Mg(NO₃)₂: Mg²⁺ + 2NO₃⁻ = 0, parentheses show 2 nitrate ions.

examples.explanation: Al³⁺ (Group 13) + O²⁻ (Group 16) → need 2 Al³⁺ (+6) and 3 O²⁻ (-6) → Al₂O₃. Cross-multiply charges to find subscripts.

classifications.value: Metal name + (Roman numeral) + nonmetal-ide or polyatomic name → use Roman numeral for metal charge
characteristics: Anion charge from group or memorized
Parentheses for polyatomic ions with subscript > 1
classifications.behavior: Roman numeral gives metal charge directly; balance with anion charge to determine subscripts.

examples.explanation: Fe³⁺ (from III) + Cl⁻ → need 3 Cl⁻ to balance Fe³⁺ → FeCl₃. Roman numeral provides Fe charge.

image.caption: FeCl₃: Fe³⁺ + 3Cl⁻ = 0, Roman numeral gives metal charge.

examples.explanation: Cu²⁺ (from II) + SO₄²⁻ → charges balance 1:1 → CuSO₄. No parentheses needed (only 1 sulfate).

image.caption: CuSO₄: Cu²⁺ + SO₄²⁻ = 0, 1:1 ratio.

examples.explanation: Pb⁴⁺ (from IV) + O²⁻ → need 2 O²⁻ to balance Pb⁴⁺ → PbO₂. Roman numeral IV = +4 charge.

image.caption: PbO₂: Pb⁴⁺ + 2O²⁻ = 0, Roman numeral IV = +4.

classifications.value: Metal name + polyatomic ion name → balance charges, use parentheses when needed
characteristics: Parentheses around polyatomic ion when subscript > 1
classifications.behavior: Treat polyatomic ion as single unit with fixed charge; balance with metal cation charge.

examples.explanation: NH₄⁺ (memorized) + PO₄³⁻ (memorized) → need 3 NH₄⁺ to balance PO₄³⁻ → (NH₄)₃PO₄. Parentheses around NH₄ because subscript 3 applies to entire ion.

image.caption: (NH₄)₃PO₄: 3 NH₄⁺ (+3) + PO₄³⁻ (-3) = 0.

examples.explanation: Ca²⁺ (Group 2) + OH⁻ (memorized) → need 2 OH⁻ to balance Ca²⁺ → Ca(OH)₂. Parentheses around OH because subscript 2 applies to entire ion.

image.caption: Ca(OH)₂: Ca²⁺ + 2OH⁻ = 0, parentheses show 2 hydroxide ions.

examples.explanation: Al³⁺ (Group 13) + SO₄²⁻ (memorized) → need 2 Al³⁺ (+6) and 3 SO₄²⁻ (-6) → Al₂(SO₄)₃. Parentheses around SO₄ because subscript 3 applies to entire ion.

image.caption: Al₂(SO₄)₃: 2 Al³⁺ (+6) + 3 SO₄²⁻ (-6) = 0.

principles.statement: A shortcut for charge balance: write ion charges as superscripts, then 'criss-cross' them to become subscripts (reducing if possible).
principles.application: Al³⁺ O²⁻ → criss-cross: Al₂O₃. Mg²⁺ NO₃⁻ → criss-cross: Mg(NO₃)₂. Fe³⁺ SO₄²⁻ → criss-cross: Fe₂(SO₄)₃.
principles.example: Name: calcium phosphide. Analysis: Ca²⁺ + P³⁻ → criss-cross: Ca₃P₂. Verify: 3(+2) + 2(-3) = 0 ✓.
principles.analogy: Like swapping seats: cation charge becomes anion subscript, anion charge becomes cation subscript.

image.alt: Diagram showing criss-cross method for ionic formula writing
image.caption: Criss-cross method: ion charges become subscripts for opposite ion.

principles.statement: Use parentheses around a polyatomic ion formula only when its subscript is greater than 1; never use parentheses for monatomic ions.
principles.application: Correct: Mg(NO₃)₂ (parentheses for 2 nitrate ions), Ca(OH)₂ (parentheses for 2 hydroxide ions). Incorrect: MgNO₃₂, CaOH₂ (missing parentheses).
principles.example: Name: aluminum nitrate. Analysis: Al³⁺ + NO₃⁻ → need 3 NO₃⁻ → Al(NO₃)₃. Parentheses show subscript 3 applies to entire NO₃ unit, not just O.

image.alt: Examples showing correct parentheses usage for polyatomic ions
image.caption: Parentheses rule: use around polyatomic ion when subscript > 1.

components.equation: $\text{Formula} = \text{Cation}_{\text{subscript}} + \text{Anion}_{\text{subscript}} \text{ where subscripts balance charges to zero}$
components.interpretation: Identify ions and charges from name; use criss-cross or algebraic method to find subscripts; use parentheses for polyatomic ions with subscript > 1.

worked_examples.title: Writing Ionic Formulas from Names
worked_examples.problem: Write formulas for: (a) potassium sulfide, (b) iron(II) nitrate, (c) aluminum phosphate, (d) ammonium carbonate, (e) copper(I) oxide.

step_by_step.calculation: K⁺ (Group 1) + S²⁻ (Group 16) → need 2 K⁺ to balance S²⁻ → K₂S
Fe²⁺ (from II) + NO₃⁻ → need 2 NO₃⁻ to balance Fe²⁺ → Fe(NO₃)₂ (parentheses needed)
Al³⁺ (Group 13) + PO₄³⁻ → charges balance 1:1 → AlPO₄ (no parentheses needed)
NH₄⁺ + CO₃²⁻ → need 2 NH₄⁺ to balance CO₃²⁻ → (NH₄)₂CO₃ (parentheses for NH₄)
Cu⁺ (from I) + O²⁻ → need 2 Cu⁺ to balance O²⁻ → Cu₂O

worked_examples.final_answer: (a) K₂S, (b) Fe(NO₃)₂, (c) AlPO₄, (d) (NH₄)₂CO₃, (e) Cu₂O. All formulas from charge balance: Type I metals use group number for charge, Type II use Roman numeral, polyatomic ions memorized, parentheses when subscript > 1 for polyatomic ions.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Writing ionic compound formulas using charge balance and parentheses rules

sections.introduction: Covalent compound formula writing is more straightforward than ionic: Greek prefixes in the name directly indicate the number of atoms of each element, requiring no charge balancing.

key_concept.definition: For covalent compounds: (1) Identify both elements from name, (2) Convert Greek prefixes to subscripts (mono- = 1, omit subscript; di- = ₂, tri- = ₃, etc.), (3) Write element symbols in name order with subscripts, (4) Omit subscript 1.
key_concept.context: Unlike ionic compounds, covalent formulas don't require charge balancing because prefixes specify exact atom counts. The first element omits mono- prefix in name but still represents 1 atom in formula.

principles.statement: Greek prefixes in covalent compound names convert directly to subscripts: mono- = 1 (omit subscript), di- = ₂, tri- = ₃, tetra- = ₄, penta- = ₅, hexa- = ₆, etc.
principles.application: carbon dioxide: no prefix on C = 1 C, di- = 2 O → CO₂. dinitrogen tetroxide: di- = 2 N, tetra- = 4 O → N₂O₄.
principles.example: Name: phosphorus pentachloride. Analysis: no prefix on P = 1 P, penta- = 5 Cl → PCl₅. Prefixes give exact atom counts.

image.alt: Table showing direct prefix to subscript conversion
image.caption: Direct conversion: prefix value = subscript (omit 1).

principles.statement: Write element symbols in the same order as they appear in the name: first element first, second element second.
principles.application: nitrogen trifluoride: nitrogen first, fluorine second → NF₃ (not F₃N). sulfur dioxide: sulfur first, oxygen second → SO₂.
principles.example: Name: dichlorine monoxide. Analysis: chlorine first, oxygen second, di- = 2 Cl, mono- = 1 O → Cl₂O (not OCl₂).

image.alt: Examples showing element order in covalent formulas
image.caption: Element order: name order = formula order for covalent compounds.

components.interpretation: Prefix directly gives subscript; omit subscript 1; element order matches name order; no charge balancing needed.

worked_examples.title: Writing Covalent Formulas from Names
worked_examples.problem: Write formulas for: (a) carbon monoxide, (b) sulfur trioxide, (c) dinitrogen pentoxide, (d) phosphorus trichloride, (e) silicon tetrachloride.

step_by_step.calculation: C (no prefix = 1) + mon- O (1) → CO (omit subscript 1)
S (no prefix = 1) + tri- O (3) → SO₃
di- N (2) + pent- O (5, vowel dropped) → N₂O₅
P (no prefix = 1) + tri- Cl (3) → PCl₃
Si (no prefix = 1) + tetra- Cl (4) → SiCl₄

worked_examples.final_answer: (a) CO, (b) SO₃, (c) N₂O₅, (d) PCl₃, (e) SiCl₄. Prefixes directly give subscripts; omit subscript 1; element order matches name order. No charge balancing needed for covalent compounds.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Writing covalent compound formulas using prefix-to-subscript conversion

sections.section_title: Writing Formulas for Acids and Bases
sections.introduction: Acid and base formula writing combines ionic compound rules with special patterns for acids. Recognizing acid naming patterns (hydro- for binary, -ic/-ous for oxyacids) is essential for correct formula writing.

key_concept.definition: For acids: binary acids (hydro-...-ic) use H⁺ + nonmetal anion; oxyacids (-ic/-ous) use H⁺ + polyatomic oxyanion. For bases: metal + hydroxide, named like ionic compounds.
key_concept.context: Acid formulas always start with H (written first). Binary acids: hydro- prefix indicates no oxygen. Oxyacids: -ic from -ate anion, -ous from -ite anion. Bases: OH⁻ is the polyatomic anion.

classifications.value: hydro- + nonmetal root + -ic acid → H⁺ + nonmetal anion
classifications.behavior: hydro- prefix indicates binary acid (no oxygen); balance H⁺ (+1) with anion charge.

examples.explanation: hydro- + chlor + -ic → binary acid. Cl⁻ (chloride) + H⁺ → HCl. Charges balance 1:1.

image.caption: HCl: H⁺ + Cl⁻ = 0, binary acid pattern.

examples.explanation: hydro- + sulfur + -ic → binary acid. S²⁻ (sulfide) + 2H⁺ → H₂S. Need 2 H⁺ to balance S²⁻.

image.caption: H₂S: 2H⁺ + S²⁻ = 0, binary acid.

characteristics: Name ends in '-ic acid' or '-ous acid'
classifications.behavior: -ic acid from -ate anion, -ous acid from -ite anion; balance H⁺ with polyatomic anion charge.

examples.explanation: -ic acid → -ate anion. Nitric → nitrate (NO₃⁻). H⁺ + NO₃⁻ → HNO₃. Charges balance 1:1.

image.caption: HNO₃: H⁺ + NO₃⁻ = 0, oxyacid from nitrate.

examples.explanation: -ous acid → -ite anion. Sulfurous → sulfite (SO₃²⁻). 2H⁺ + SO₃²⁻ → H₂SO₃. Need 2 H⁺ to balance SO₃²⁻.

image.caption: H₂SO₃: 2H⁺ + SO₃²⁻ = 0, oxyacid from sulfite.

examples.explanation: -ic acid → -ate anion. Phosphoric → phosphate (PO₄³⁻). 3H⁺ + PO₄³⁻ → H₃PO₄. Need 3 H⁺ to balance PO₄³⁻.

image.caption: H₃PO₄: 3H⁺ + PO₄³⁻ = 0, oxyacid from phosphate.

classifications.value: Metal name + hydroxide → metal cation + OH⁻
classifications.behavior: Bases are ionic compounds with OH⁻ anion; write formula like Type I or Type II ionic compound.

examples.explanation: Na⁺ (Group 1) + OH⁻ → charges balance 1:1 → NaOH. No parentheses needed (only 1 OH⁻).

image.caption: NaOH: Na⁺ + OH⁻ = 0, base formula.

examples.explanation: Ca²⁺ (Group 2) + OH⁻ → need 2 OH⁻ to balance Ca²⁺ → Ca(OH)₂. Parentheses around OH because subscript 2 applies to entire ion.

image.caption: Ca(OH)₂: Ca²⁺ + 2OH⁻ = 0, parentheses show 2 hydroxide ions.

examples.explanation: Fe³⁺ (from III) + OH⁻ → need 3 OH⁻ to balance Fe³⁺ → Fe(OH)₃. Roman numeral provides Fe charge.

image.caption: Fe(OH)₃: Fe³⁺ + 3OH⁻ = 0, Type II base.

principles.statement: Acid formulas always start with H; binary acids: H + nonmetal; oxyacids: H + polyatomic oxyanion; subscripts balance H⁺ (+1) with anion charge.
principles.application: hydrobromic acid: H⁺ + Br⁻ → HBr. nitrous acid: H⁺ + NO₂⁻ → HNO₂. sulfuric acid: 2H⁺ + SO₄²⁻ → H₂SO₄.
principles.example: Name: perchloric acid. Analysis: -ic acid → -ate anion. Perchloric → perchlorate (ClO₄⁻). H⁺ + ClO₄⁻ → HClO₄.

image.caption: Acid formulas: H first, balance H⁺ with anion charge.

principles.statement: Use parentheses around OH in base formulas only when subscript > 1; never use parentheses for monatomic cations.
principles.application: Correct: Ca(OH)₂ (parentheses for 2 OH⁻), NaOH (no parentheses, 1 OH⁻). Incorrect: CaOH₂ (missing parentheses).
principles.example: Name: aluminum hydroxide. Analysis: Al³⁺ + OH⁻ → need 3 OH⁻ → Al(OH)₃. Parentheses show subscript 3 applies to entire OH unit.

image.caption: Base parentheses: use around OH when subscript > 1.

components.definition: Acid and base formula writing rules
components.interpretation: Acids: H written first, subscripts balance H⁺ with anion. Bases: metal + OH⁻, parentheses when subscript > 1 for OH.

worked_examples.title: Writing Acid and Base Formulas from Names
worked_examples.problem: Write formulas for: (a) hydroiodic acid, (b) nitric acid, (c) sulfurous acid, (d) potassium hydroxide, (e) iron(II) hydroxide.

step_by_step.calculation: hydro- = binary acid. I⁻ (iodide) + H⁺ → HI. Charges balance 1:1.
-ic acid = -ate anion. Nitrate (NO₃⁻) + H⁺ → HNO₃. Charges balance 1:1.
-ous acid = -ite anion. Sulfite (SO₃²⁻) + 2H⁺ → H₂SO₃. Need 2 H⁺ to balance SO₃²⁻.
K⁺ (Group 1) + OH⁻ → KOH. Charges balance 1:1, no parentheses needed.
Fe²⁺ (from II) + OH⁻ → Fe(OH)₂. Need 2 OH⁻ to balance Fe²⁺, parentheses needed.

worked_examples.final_answer: (a) HI, (b) HNO₃, (c) H₂SO₃, (d) KOH, (e) Fe(OH)₂. Binary acids: hydro- + nonmetal. Oxyacids: -ic from -ate, -ous from -ite. Bases: metal + OH⁻ with parentheses when subscript > 1.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Writing acid and base formulas using naming pattern recognition and charge balance

sections.introduction: Hydrate formula writing combines ionic compound rules with dot notation for water molecules. The Greek prefix in the hydrate name directly indicates the number of water molecules.

key_concept.definition: For hydrates: (1) Write the ionic compound formula using standard rules, (2) Add a centered dot (·), (3) Write the number of water molecules as subscript before H₂O using the Greek prefix from the name.
key_concept.context: The dot (·) indicates water molecules are associated with but not chemically bonded to the ionic compound. The subscript applies only to H₂O, not to the entire formula.

principles.statement: Hydrate formula = [Ionic compound formula] · nH₂O where n = Greek prefix value from name (mono- = 1, di- = 2, tri- = 3, etc.).
principles.application: copper(II) sulfate pentahydrate: CuSO₄ (ionic) + penta- = 5 → CuSO₄·5H₂O. calcium chloride dihydrate: CaCl₂ + di- = 2 → CaCl₂·2H₂O.
principles.example: Name: magnesium sulfate heptahydrate. Analysis: MgSO₄ (ionic) + hepta- = 7 → MgSO₄·7H₂O. Dot separates ionic compound from water.

image.caption: Hydrate formula: ionic compound · nH₂O, n from Greek prefix.

principles.statement: Use a centered dot (·) to separate ionic compound from water molecules; never use period (.) or multiplication sign (×); no parentheses around H₂O.
principles.application: Correct: CuSO₄·5H₂O. Incorrect: CuSO₄.5H₂O (period), CuSO₄×5H₂O (multiplication), CuSO₄·(5H₂O) (parentheses around water).
principles.example: Name: sodium carbonate decahydrate. Analysis: Na₂CO₃ + deca- = 10 → Na₂CO₃·10H₂O. Dot shows association, not bonding.

image.caption: Dot notation: centered dot (·) separates ionic compound from water.

components.interpretation: Write ionic formula first, add centered dot, then nH₂O with subscript n. Dot indicates association, not chemical bond.

worked_examples.title: Writing Hydrate Formulas from Names
worked_examples.problem: Write formulas for: (a) barium chloride dihydrate, (b) sodium sulfate decahydrate, (c) cobalt(II) chloride hexahydrate, (d) aluminum nitrate nonahydrate.

step_by_step.explanation: Write formula for barium chloride dihydrate.
step_by_step.calculation: BaCl₂ (Ba²⁺ + 2Cl⁻) + di- = 2 → BaCl₂·2H₂O

step_by_step.explanation: Write formula for sodium sulfate decahydrate.
step_by_step.calculation: Na₂SO₄ (2Na⁺ + SO₄²⁻) + deca- = 10 → Na₂SO₄·10H₂O

step_by_step.explanation: Write formula for cobalt(II) chloride hexahydrate.
step_by_step.calculation: CoCl₂ (Co²⁺ + 2Cl⁻) + hexa- = 6 → CoCl₂·6H₂O

step_by_step.explanation: Write formula for aluminum nitrate nonahydrate.
step_by_step.calculation: Al(NO₃)₃ (Al³⁺ + 3NO₃⁻) + nona- = 9 → Al(NO₃)₃·9H₂O

worked_examples.final_answer: (a) BaCl₂·2H₂O, (b) Na₂SO₄·10H₂O, (c) CoCl₂·6H₂O, (d) Al(NO₃)₃·9H₂O. All use centered dot (·), correct Greek prefix conversion, and proper ionic compound formulas.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Writing hydrate formulas using ionic rules + dot notation + prefix-to-subscript conversion

sections.section_title: Common Errors and How to Avoid Them
sections.introduction: Formula writing has specific rules that differ by compound type. Recognizing common errors and learning correction strategies builds accuracy and prevents confusion between naming systems.

key_concept.definition: Common formula writing errors include: incorrect charge balance for ionic compounds, wrong prefix conversion for covalent compounds, misidentifying acid type, missing parentheses for polyatomic ions, and incorrect dot notation for hydrates.
key_concept.context: A verification checklist prevents errors: compound type identified correctly? Charges balanced (ionic)? Prefixes converted correctly (covalent)? Acid pattern followed? Parentheses used when needed? Dot notation correct for hydrates?

classifications.type: Incorrect Charge Balance for Ionic Compounds
classifications.value: Not balancing total positive and negative charges to zero
classifications.behavior: Results in formulas that don't represent neutral compounds; total charge ≠ 0.

examples.explanation: Incorrect: AlO for aluminum oxide. Correct: Al₂O₃. Al³⁺ + O²⁻ needs 2 Al³⁺ (+6) and 3 O²⁻ (-6) to balance.

image.caption: Al₂O₃ not AlO; need 2 Al³⁺ (+6) and 3 O²⁻ (-6) for charge balance.

examples.explanation: Incorrect: NaSO₄ for sodium sulfate. Correct: Na₂SO₄. Na⁺ + SO₄²⁻ needs 2 Na⁺ (+2) to balance 1 SO₄²⁻ (-2).

image.caption: Na₂SO₄ not NaSO₄; need 2 Na⁺ to balance 1 SO₄²⁻.

classifications.type: Wrong Prefix Conversion for Covalent Compounds
classifications.value: Incorrectly converting Greek prefixes to subscripts
classifications.behavior: Results in wrong formulas: CO is carbon monoxide, not carbon dioxide; prefixes must match atom counts exactly.

examples.explanation: Incorrect: CO for carbon dioxide. Correct: CO₂. di- = 2 oxygen atoms, not 1.

image.caption: CO₂ not CO; di- = 2 oxygen atoms.

examples.explanation: Incorrect: N₂O₃ for dinitrogen pentoxide. Correct: N₂O₅. penta- = 5 oxygen atoms, not 3.

image.caption: N₂O₅ not N₂O₃; penta- = 5 oxygen atoms.

classifications.value: Confusing binary acids with oxyacids, leading to wrong anion
classifications.behavior: Results in completely wrong compounds: HCl (hydrochloric acid) vs. HClO (hypochlorous acid) are different substances.

examples.explanation: Incorrect: HClO for hydrochloric acid. Correct: HCl. 'hydro-' indicates binary acid (no oxygen), so anion is Cl⁻, not ClO⁻.

image.caption: HCl not HClO; hydro- = binary acid (no oxygen).

examples.explanation: Incorrect: HCl for chloric acid. Correct: HClO₃. No 'hydro-' means oxyacid; chloric → chlorate (ClO₃⁻).

image.caption: HClO₃ not HCl; chloric acid is oxyacid from chlorate.

classifications.value: Forgetting parentheses around polyatomic ions when subscript > 1
classifications.behavior: Results in incorrect formulas: MgNO₃₂ suggests 1 Mg, 1 N, 6 O (wrong); Mg(NO₃)₂ correctly shows 1 Mg, 2 N, 6 O.

examples.explanation: Incorrect: MgNO₃₂ for magnesium nitrate. Correct: Mg(NO₃)₂. Parentheses show subscript 2 applies to entire NO₃ unit, not just O.

image.caption: Mg(NO₃)₂ not MgNO₃₂; parentheses show 2 complete nitrate ions.

examples.explanation: Incorrect: CaOH₂ for calcium hydroxide. Correct: Ca(OH)₂. Without parentheses, OH₂ suggests 1 O and 2 H, not 2 OH⁻ ions.

image.caption: Ca(OH)₂ not CaOH₂; parentheses show 2 complete hydroxide ions.

classifications.value: Using wrong symbol or format for hydrate dot notation
classifications.behavior: Results in ambiguous or incorrect formulas; dot notation is standardized for hydrates.

examples.explanation: Incorrect: CuSO₄.5H₂O (period). Correct: CuSO₄·5H₂O (centered dot). Period suggests decimal; dot indicates hydration.

image.caption: Hydrate formulas: use centered dot (·), not period (.) or other symbols.

principles.statement: Always verify formulas by checking: (1) Compound type identified correctly, (2) Charges balanced to zero (ionic), (3) Prefixes converted correctly (covalent), (4) Acid pattern followed (binary vs. oxyacid), (5) Parentheses used when needed for polyatomic ions, (6) Dot notation correct for hydrates.
principles.application: Prevents common errors; systematic verification builds accuracy habit for laboratory work and chemical communication.
principles.example: Formula: Fe₂(SO₄)₃. Check: Type II ionic (iron(III)) ✓, Fe³⁺ + SO₄²⁻ balanced (2×+3 + 3×-2 = 0) ✓, parentheses for SO₄ with subscript 3 ✓. All checks pass.

image.caption: Formula checklist: compound type, charge balance, prefix conversion, acid pattern, parentheses, dot notation.

principles.statement: After writing a formula, convert it back to a name to verify accuracy; the regenerated name should match the original name.
principles.application: Write Fe₂(SO₄)₃ from 'iron(III) sulfate'; convert back: Fe³⁺ + SO₄²⁻ → iron(III) sulfate ✓. Mismatch indicates error.
principles.example: Name: dinitrogen tetroxide → N₂O₄. Convert back: N₂ = dinitrogen, O₄ = tetroxide → dinitrogen tetroxide ✓. Match confirms accuracy.

image.alt: Cross-checking formula by converting back to name
image.caption: Cross-check: write formula from name, then convert back to verify match.

worked_examples.title: Error Identification and Correction
worked_examples.problem: Identify the error in each formula and provide the correct version: (a) AlO for aluminum oxide, (b) CO for carbon dioxide, (c) HClO for hydrochloric acid, (d) MgNO₃₂ for magnesium nitrate, (e) CuSO₄.5H₂O for copper(II) sulfate pentahydrate.

step_by_step.calculation: Error: charges don't balance. Al³⁺ + O²⁻ ≠ 0. Correction: Need 2 Al³⁺ (+6) and 3 O²⁻ (-6). Correct formula: Al₂O₃.
Error: prefix conversion wrong. di- = 2, not 1. Correction: carbon dioxide has 2 O atoms. Correct formula: CO₂.
Error: misidentified acid type. 'hydro-' = binary acid (no O). Correction: HCl = H⁺ + Cl⁻. Correct formula: HCl.
Error: missing parentheses. Subscript 2 applies to entire NO₃ unit. Correction: Mg(NO₃)₂ shows 2 complete nitrate ions.

step_by_step.explanation: Analyze CuSO₄.5H₂O for copper(II) sulfate pentahydrate.
step_by_step.calculation: Error: period instead of centered dot. Correction: Use centered dot (·) for hydration. Correct formula: CuSO₄·5H₂O.

worked_examples.final_answer: (a) AlO → Al₂O₃ (balance charges: 2 Al³⁺ + 3 O²⁻ = 0). (b) CO → CO₂ (di- = 2 O atoms). (c) HClO → HCl (hydro- = binary acid, no oxygen). (d) MgNO₃₂ → Mg(NO₃)₂ (parentheses for polyatomic ion with subscript > 1). (e) CuSO₄.5H₂O → CuSO₄·5H₂O (centered dot for hydrate). Systematic verification catches these common formula writing errors.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Error identification and correction using formula writing verification checklist

sections.introduction: Formula writing mastery requires active practice with all compound types. Work through these problems to build confidence and accuracy in converting systematic names to correct chemical formulas.

key_concept.definition: Practice problems cover: identifying compound type from name, writing formulas for ionic compounds (Type I and II), covalent compounds, acids, bases, and hydrates, and correcting common errors.
key_concept.context: Active recall and immediate feedback reinforce pattern recognition; focus on charge balance for ionic, prefix conversion for covalent, and acid/base pattern recognition.

worked_examples.title: Comprehensive Formula Writing Practice Set
worked_examples.problem: Write formulas for: (1) potassium bromide, (2) iron(III) sulfide, (3) dinitrogen trioxide, (4) hydrobromic acid, (5) phosphoric acid, (6) calcium hydroxide, (7) ammonium phosphate, (8) copper(II) sulfate pentahydrate, (9) sodium carbonate decahydrate, (10) magnesium chloride hexahydrate.

step_by_step.calculation: K⁺ (Group 1) + Br⁻ (Group 17) → KBr (charges balance 1:1)
Fe³⁺ (from III) + S²⁻ → need 2 Fe³⁺ (+6) and 3 S²⁻ (-6) → Fe₂S₃
Covalent: di- = 2 N, tri- = 3 O → N₂O₃
Binary acid: H⁺ + Br⁻ → HBr
Oxyacid (-ic): phosphate (PO₄³⁻) + 3H⁺ → H₃PO₄
Ca²⁺ + OH⁻ → need 2 OH⁻ → Ca(OH)₂ (parentheses needed)
NH₄⁺ + PO₄³⁻ → need 3 NH₄⁺ → (NH₄)₃PO₄ (parentheses for NH₄)

step_by_step.explanation: Write formula for copper(II) sulfate pentahydrate.
step_by_step.calculation: Cu²⁺ + SO₄²⁻ → CuSO₄ + penta- = 5 → CuSO₄·5H₂O

step_by_step.explanation: Write formula for sodium carbonate decahydrate.
step_by_step.calculation: 2Na⁺ + CO₃²⁻ → Na₂CO₃ + deca- = 10 → Na₂CO₃·10H₂O

step_by_step.explanation: Write formula for magnesium chloride hexahydrate.
step_by_step.calculation: Mg²⁺ + 2Cl⁻ → MgCl₂ + hexa- = 6 → MgCl₂·6H₂O

worked_examples.final_answer: (1) KBr, (2) Fe₂S₃, (3) N₂O₃, (4) HBr, (5) H₃PO₄, (6) Ca(OH)₂, (7) (NH₄)₃PO₄, (8) CuSO₄·5H₂O, (9) Na₂CO₃·10H₂O, (10) MgCl₂·6H₂O. All formulas from correct compound type identification, charge balance (ionic), prefix conversion (covalent), acid/base patterns, and hydrate dot notation.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Comprehensive formula writing practice covering all compound types

worked_examples.title: Formula Writing Challenge: Mixed Compound Types
worked_examples.problem: Write formulas for these challenging names: (a) lead(IV) oxide, (b) dichlorine heptoxide, (c) hypochlorous acid, (d) barium hydroxide octahydrate, (e) iron(II) ammonium sulfate hexahydrate.

step_by_step.calculation: Pb⁴⁺ (from IV) + O²⁻ → need 2 O²⁻ to balance Pb⁴⁺ → PbO₂
Covalent: di- = 2 Cl, hepta- = 7 O → Cl₂O₇ (vowel drop: heptoxide)
Oxyacid (-ous): hypochlorite (ClO⁻) + H⁺ → HClO

step_by_step.explanation: Write formula for barium hydroxide octahydrate.
step_by_step.calculation: Ba²⁺ + 2OH⁻ → Ba(OH)₂ + octa- = 8 → Ba(OH)₂·8H₂O

step_by_step.explanation: Write formula for iron(II) ammonium sulfate hexahydrate.
step_by_step.calculation: Fe²⁺ + 2NH₄⁺ + 2SO₄²⁻ → Fe(NH₄)₂(SO₄)₂ + hexa- = 6 → Fe(NH₄)₂(SO₄)₂·6H₂O

worked_examples.final_answer: (a) PbO₂, (b) Cl₂O₇, (c) HClO, (d) Ba(OH)₂·8H₂O, (e) Fe(NH₄)₂(SO₄)₂·6H₂O. Complex formulas require careful identification of compound type, charge balance, prefix conversion, and proper notation (parentheses, dot).
worked_examples.concept_applied: Writing formulas for complex names with multiple components and special cases

summary: Writing formulas from names is the reverse of nomenclature: identify compound type from name patterns, then apply appropriate rules. Name patterns reveal type: Roman numerals = Type II ionic (use numeral for metal charge); Greek prefixes = covalent (prefixes → subscripts); 'hydro-' = binary acid (H⁺ + nonmetal); '-ic/-ous acid' = oxyacid (H⁺ + polyatomic oxyanion); 'hydrate' = hydrate (ionic · nH₂O). Ionic formulas: balance cation and anion charges to zero (criss-cross method); use parentheses for polyatomic ions with subscript > 1. Covalent formulas: prefixes directly give subscripts (omit subscript 1); element order matches name order. Acid formulas: always start with H; binary: H⁺ + nonmetal anion; oxyacids: H⁺ + polyatomic oxyanion (-ic from -ate, -ous from -ite). Base formulas: metal + OH⁻, parentheses when subscript > 1 for OH. Hydrate formulas: ionic compound + centered dot (·) + nH₂O (n from prefix). Common errors: incorrect charge balance (ionic), wrong prefix conversion (covalent), misidentifying acid type, missing parentheses for polyatomic ions, incorrect dot notation for hydrates. Verification checklist: compound type correct? Charges balanced? Prefixes converted? Acid pattern followed? Parentheses used? Dot notation correct? Cross-check by converting formula back to name. Mastery of formula writing essential for laboratory work, chemical calculations, and accurate chemical communication.
